name: La Puebla - Polán - San Martín
name_original: Embalse de Castrejón y Polán – circular semi gravel desde La Puebla de Montalbán
description: This 43.5-mile semi-gravel road cycling route near La Puebla de Montalbán features the stunning Barrancas de Burujón and mixed terrain.
region_intro: Road cycling around La Puebla de Montalbán features routes through the plains of the Toledo province, often following rural roads that connect historic towns and villages. The landscape is characterized by open fields, gentle undulations, and occasional river crossings, notably over the Tagus. Elevations are generally moderate, making the area suitable for various road cycling abilities. Best road cycling routes around La Puebla de Montalbán The most popular road cycling route is La Puebla - Polán - San Martín, a 43.5 miles (70.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 59 minutes to complete. This moderate route connects several local towns, offering a comprehensive tour of the area. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Celestina Museum – Bridge over the Tagus loop from La Puebla de Montalbán, a moderate 20.3 miles (32.7 km) path. This route includes cultural points of interest and scenic views along the Tagus river. Local road cyclists also love the Roadbike loop from La Mata, a 16.5 miles (26.5 km) trail leading through the local countryside, often completed in about 1 hour 10 minutes. Road cycling around La Puebla de Montalbán is defined by rural roads, river crossings, and open agricultural landscapes. The network offers options for easy and moderate ability levels. The routes in La Puebla de Montalbán are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 80 road cyclists have used komoot to explore La Puebla de Montalbán's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in La Puebla de Montalbán?
A: There are 7 road cycling routes available around La Puebla de Montalbán, catering to various skill levels from easy to moderate.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in La Puebla de Montalbán?
A: Yes, the region offers 2 easy routes perfect for beginners. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38169602" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roadbike loop from La Mata&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 16.5-mile (26.5 km) trail that takes about 1 hour 10 minutes, featuring open fields and gentle terrain.

Q: What are some longer road cycling routes available around La Puebla de Montalbán?
A: For longer rides, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029247697/la-puebla-polan-san-martin" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Puebla - Polán - San Martín&lt;/a&gt; route, which spans 43.5 miles (70.0 km) and offers a comprehensive tour connecting several local towns.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes in La Puebla de Montalbán are designed as loops. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39555249" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Celestina Museum – Bridge over the Tagus loop from La Puebla de Montalbán&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 20.3-mile (32.7 km) path that includes cultural points of interest and scenic river views.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on road cycling routes in La Puebla de Montalbán?
A: Road cycling routes in La Puebla de Montalbán typically feature the plains of the Toledo province, characterized by open agricultural landscapes, rural roads, and occasional river crossings, notably over the Tagus. You'll experience gentle undulations rather than steep climbs.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in La Puebla de Montalbán?
A: The spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in La Puebla de Montalbán, with milder temperatures. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cool, though cycling is possible year-round.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical landmarks to see along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, some routes incorporate cultural points of interest. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39555280" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Celestina Museum – Malpica Castle loop from La Puebla de Montalbán&lt;/a&gt; allows you to pass by historical sites like Malpica Castle.

Q: What natural attractions can be found near the road cycling routes?
A: The region offers several natural highlights. You can find viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/614479" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Enebros Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/614477" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Barrancas de Burujón and the Castrejón Reservoir from Mirador del Cambrón&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6218300" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Carpio&lt;/a&gt; is also a notable lake in the vicinity.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in La Puebla de Montalbán?
A: The road cycling routes in La Puebla de Montalbán are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.0 stars. Cyclists often appreciate the quiet rural roads and the opportunity to explore the local towns and river landscapes.

Q: Are there any moderate difficulty road cycling routes for more experienced riders?
A: Yes, there are 5 moderate routes available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31430240" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Malpica Castle – Bridge over the Tagus loop from El Carpio de Tajo&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 23.6-mile (38.1 km) route that offers a good challenge with varied terrain.

Q: Where do most road cycling routes typically start in La Puebla de Montalbán?
A: Many road cycling routes in the area, such as the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029247697/la-puebla-polan-san-martin" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Puebla - Polán - San Martín&lt;/a&gt;, often start directly from La Puebla de Montalbán itself, or from nearby towns like La Mata or El Carpio de Tajo, making them easily accessible.
tips: Still on the arid plateau but close to the exuberant Vega del Tajo is this town, proud to be the birthplace of the author Fernando de Rojas, to whom the Celestina Museum is dedicated. It is worth, at least, going through its main square.

Quiet road that slopes up from Las Barrancas. Pavement in poor condition, ideal for gravel.
Quiet road, better to ride uphill from Burujon to Polan. Ideal for gravel biking

Polán Castle is a medieval fortification located in the municipality of Polán, about 15 km southwest of Toledo, in the Montes de Toledo region.

It is situated within the town itself, nestled among houses and courtyards, giving it a unique character.

Built between the 11th and 12th centuries, it was likely used as a defensive tower after the Reconquista of Toledo (1085).

Although the town's biggest attractions, like its castle and Roman bridge, are all on the outskirts and not reachable by road bike, the town itself has a pretty church dedicated to Saint Andrew and a couple of good bars. for a stop

faqs.question: Is this 'semi gravel' route suitable for a standard road bike?
faqs.answer: This route is designated as 'semi gravel' for a racebike, meaning it includes a mix of asphalt roads and unpaved dirt tracks. While some unpaved sections might be smooth, others could be challenging with loose earth, stones, or irregular surfaces. For a conventional road bike, it's recommended to have wider, more robust tires (e.g., 28mm or wider, with some tread) to enhance comfort and safety and reduce the risk of punctures. Gravel bikes are explicitly recommended for such mixed routes.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: The most striking scenic feature is the spectacular Barrancas de Burujón, often likened to a 'Colorado Canyon of Toledo,' with its red clay cliffs formed by the Tagus river. You'll find impressive views from viewpoints like Mirador del Cambrón and Mirador de los Enebros. Other points of interest include the La Celestina Museum, Polán Castle, and the Church of San Andrés, San Martín de Montalbán.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route near La Puebla de Montalbán?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route aren't detailed, La Puebla de Montalbán, as the starting point, offers various public parking options within the town. Look for available street parking or designated lots in the town center to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The area around Embalse de Castrejón y Polán is best enjoyed during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, making cycling less comfortable, especially on exposed sections. Winter can be pleasant on sunny days, but be prepared for cooler temperatures and potentially muddy unpaved sections after rain.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the route?
faqs.answer: This route primarily uses public roads and tracks. While there are no strict prohibitions against dogs, it's generally advisable to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through populated areas or near wildlife habitats. Remember to always clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and tracks around the Embalse de Castrejón y Polán and through the Barrancas de Burujón area, which is a protected natural space but freely accessible.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the CM-4050 section between the Castrejón Reservoir and Polán?
faqs.answer: The CM-4050 between the Castrejón Reservoir and Polán is specifically highlighted as a gravel section. This means you should expect unpaved surfaces, which could range from well-maintained rural roads to potentially rougher tracks with loose stones or an irregular surface. It's a key part of the 'semi gravel' nature of this route.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with any other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of several other paths. You'll encounter parts of the Camino Natural del Tajo, Camino Viejo de Ventosilla, Cañada Real Segoviana, and Camino Nuevo a Menasalbas, among others, for short stretches.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter along the way?
faqs.answer: The area, particularly around the Barrancas de Burujón, is a protected natural space designated as a Wildlife Refuge and Natural Monument, and part of the Natura 2000 network for bird protection. This means you have a good chance of spotting various bird species. Keep an eye out for local fauna, especially in the quieter, more natural sections.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in La Puebla de Montalbán, where you'll find various services, including restaurants and cafes. Polán, another town on the route, also offers options for refreshments. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the sections between towns.
